labels.name: duplicate
labels.description: This issue or pull request already exists

labels.name: wontfix
labels.description: This will not be worked on

issues.title: How to define number of metadata items in a message ?
issues.author: EpicKiwi
issues.body:
Section 7.2 says that

> If the 'M' flag is set in the Message header, the header is followed by one or more Metadata Items.

So 'M' flags indicates us if there is metadata items or not. But I can't find a way to compute the number of metadata items in a message. How to know if there is a second metadata item after the first or if it is the beginning of message content ? Maybe I'm missing something.

comments.author: ricktaylor
comments.body: I have added an extra bit flag to each metadata item (now called Hint Item), to indicate if there is another Hint following.  This should fix the issue

issues.title: Metadata field may increase protocol complexity
issues.author: EpicKiwi
issues.body:
Last commits includes metadata items in every message used for transmission.
Currently, only a single metadata type is defined (the "Bundle Length Metadata Item").
In my opinion, this optional information could be included in message content of a "Transfert Start" message without adding more complexity of every message in the whole protocol.

Increasing complexity means a lot more combination case to take into account during parsing.
And some weird combinations like having a "Bundle Length Metadata Item" into a "Transfert Cancel" message.

comments.author: ricktaylor
comments.body:
That wasn't what I was trying to mandate.

All metadata items are optional - think of them as hints that MAY be added (if the flag is set).

But I understand your underlying point... the generic "here are some hints" mechanism is more complex than adding a "length" filed to the Transfer Start.

However, what if there is an out of order delivery?  It is entirely possible for the Transfer Start message to be lost and then retransmitted... perhaps a sender might include a Total Length hint in every 3rd segment?

comments.author: EpicKiwi
comments.body:
I write this issue mainly for discussing about this topic, not because I strongly think that we should not include metadata into messages.

I'm totally fine with having hints on some messages. And I think it's a good idea to give hints to help receiver collect data as fluently as possible.

Hints could be included in Transfer Start or Transfer Segment messages (for example) **content part** and leave every other messages without any mention of metadata. Those hints could be optional with the same mechanism as the one used by metadata in current state of the protocol. A flag or a length describing "metadata part".

**Potential drawback I see**: It increase the size of every messages that may contains metadata (even those with no metadata field) since we may include a flag field or a length field. But, message's length field is longer and can describe bigger messages since we don't make use of 4 bits from length field to code flags.

comments.author: ricktaylor
comments.body:
Sorry for the long silence ;)

The next revision will rename "Metadata" to "Hints" to better reflect their purpose - these are advisory items that MAY be included, not critical protocol metadata.

The key insight from our discussion was that different Hints may have different applicability. A "Bundle Length" hint only makes sense in Transfer Segment/End messages, but a future "Total Contact Time" hint might be applicable to any message type, including Padding.

Rather than restricting Hints globally to certain message types (which would limit future flexibility), the next revision will:

1. Require each Hint type specification to define which message types it's applicable to
2. Require each Hint type specification to define receiver behavior when encountered in unexpected message types

For the Bundle Length Hint specifically, the spec will state:
> This Hint Item MAY be included in a Transfer Segment Message or a Transfer End Message. Receivers SHOULD ignore this Hint Item if encountered in other message types.

This should address your concern about "weird combinations" - a Bundle Length in a Transfer Cancel would simply be ignored - while preserving flexibility for future link-level hints that might legitimately appear in any message.

The IANA registry section will mandate this pattern for all future Hint registrations.

issues.title: Monotonically decreasing segment index may lead to less compact transfers
issues.author: climoge
issues.body:
Hello. I'm trying to implement BTP Unidirectional. However I ran into a conflict between the bundle segmentation and the interleaving transfer of bundles of different priorities.

In the [Segmentation and Transfers](https://ricktaylor.github.io/btpu/draft-ietf-dtn-btpu-01/draft-ietf-dtn-btpu.html#name-segmentation-and-transfers) section, it's said that each segment is assigned a **monotonically decreasing** segment index. So when we send the first segment of a bundle we need to know the total number of segments for this bundle.

However in [Segmentation of a sequence of Bundles of different priority](https://ricktaylor.github.io/btpu/draft-ietf-dtn-btpu-01/draft-ietf-dtn-btpu.html#name-segmentation-of-a-sequence-of) it's written that segments of different bundles may be interleaved if a bundle with an higher priority is queued for transmission.

So, in the hypothetic case below :

```text
                       +----------------+
                       | Bundle C       |             High Priority
                       +----------------+
+--------------+-----------------+
| Bundle A     | Bundle B        |                    Low Priority
+--------------+-----------------+

                       :                :         
:              :                 :

+--------------+-------+----------------+-----+----------------------+
| Complete     | T1    | Complete       |  ?  |           ?          |
| Bundle A     | S0    | Bundle C       |     |                      |
+--------------+-------+----------------+-----+----------------------+

:                      :                      :                      :

+----------------------+----------------------+----------------------+
| Link-layer PDU N     | Link-layer PDU N + 1 | Link-layer PDU N + 2 |
+----------------------+----------------------+----------------------+
```

There is two possibilities to fill the link-layer PDUs.

# Possibility 1 (compatible with the current draft)

```text
                       +----------------+
                       | Bundle C       |             High Priority
                       +----------------+
+--------------+-----------------+
| Bundle A     | Bundle B        |                    Low Priority
+--------------+-----------------+

                       :                :         
:              :                 :

+--------------+-------+----------------+-----+----------+-----------+
| Complete     | T1    | Complete       | Pad |    T1    |    Pad    |
| Bundle A     | S0    | Bundle C       |     |    S1    |           |
+--------------+-------+----------------+-----+----------+-----------+

:                      :                      :                      :

+----------------------+----------------------+----------------------+
| Link-layer PDU N     | Link-layer PDU N + 1 | Link-layer PDU N + 2 |
+----------------------+----------------------+----------------------+
```

We split the `Bundle B` in two segments, the first filling up the first link-layer PDU (N). `Bundle C` with higher priority is queued, we send it as a complete bundle in the second link-layer PDU (N + 1).
The second segment of `Bundle B` doesn't fit in the remaining space of the second link-layer PDU (N + 1) so we fill it with a padding message and we send the second segment in a third link-layer PDU (N + 2).

# Possibility 2 (incompatible with the current draft but more compact)

```text
                       +----------------+
                       | Bundle C       |             High Priority
                       +----------------+
+--------------+-----------------+
| Bundle A     | Bundle B        |                    Low Priority
+--------------+-----------------+

                       :                :         
:              :                 :

+--------------+-------+----------------+-----+-----+----------------+
| Complete     | T1    | Complete       | T1  | T1  |    Pad         |
| Bundle A     | S0    | Bundle C       | S1  | S2  |                |
+--------------+-------+----------------+-----+-----+----------------+

:                      :                      :                      :

+----------------------+----------------------+----------------------+
| Link-layer PDU N     | Link-layer PDU N + 1 | Link-layer PDU N + 2 |
+----------------------+----------------------+----------------------+
```

We create a first segment for `Bundle B` to fill the first link-layer PDU (N). `Bundle C` with higher priority is queued, we send it as a complete bundle in the second link-layer PDU (N + 1).
Then we create a second segment for `Bundle B` to fill the second link-layer PDU (N + 1) and we create a third and last segment for `Bundle B` to send in the third link-layer PDU (N + 2).

# Pro and cons of each possibility

The first possibility allows to have a monotically decreasing segment index but is less data compact in certain situation (e.g: a `Bundle D` that could fit in one **bundle message** in `Link-layer PDU N + 2` of the second possibility but would need a fourth link-layer PDU (N + 3) in the first possibility).

The second possibility is the most compact and permits to fill the third link-layer PDU (N + 2) with a larger **bundle message** but we can't know in advance the total number of segments for `Bundle B` so we can't assign a monotically decreasing segment index.

# My question

Do we stick to the current **monotically decreasing segment index** or do we need to change the segment identification method to allow more compact transfers ?

There is also the possibility that I did not fully understand the draft.

comments.author: ricktaylor
comments.body:
Sorry for the late reply - you make a great deal of sense.  I'm going to revert to the original ascending index.  The descending was to satisfy a perceived need for a "expected total length" counter - but it didn't really do that well, and causes the problems you describe.

The next draft (coming soon) will revert

comments.author: climoge
comments.body: Thanks a lot ! I will try to implement the new draft in my experimental project soon.

issues.title: No way of knowing if a Bundle Message is repeated on receiver side
issues.author: climoge
issues.body:
Hello.

A `Bundle Message` does not have a `Transfer Number` field, so a **Receiver** can't determine if a received `Bundle Message` is new or is a repeated one.

Also, we can't slide the `Transfer Window` because of that, that's a problem :
In the RFC draft it's stated that we can **at most** have the `Transfer Window` size number of simultaneous transfers. But we can send an infinite amount of `Bundle Message`s because of the lack of an associated `Transfer Number`.

Or do I miss something ?

comments.author: ricktaylor
comments.body:
Hi, and thanks for reading the draft so closely — this is a very natural question coming from a bidirectional, reliable CL like TCPCLv4.

The framing to keep in mind is that BTP-U is unidirectional and unreliable by design (§Handling Link-layer PDU Loss): there's no return path, so there are no acknowledgements and no "sliding window" in the TCP flow-control/reliability sense, and deliberately repeating Messages is a feature, not something the receiver must detect and reject. With that, the two concerns resolve as follows.

1. A Bundle Message is a complete bundle, deliberately with no Transfer Number. §Segmentation and Transfers says: "if a complete Bundle can fit in the next Link-layer PDU, then the Bundle SHOULD be transferred without segmentation, see the Bundle Message." The Bundle Message (type 2) accordingly carries only the bundle itself — its content "MUST be a valid Bundle" — and has no Transfer Number field, unlike the Transfer Segment / Transfer End / Transfer Cancel Messages, which all carry one. A Transfer Number exists solely to reassemble a segmented bundle and to scope its in-progress reassembly state; a single-message bundle has nothing to reassemble, so no identifier is required. This isn't an omission — it's the draft saying a whole-bundle transfer needs no transfer context.

2. The Transfer Window bounds concurrent in-progress segmented Transfers, not the bundle rate. Per §Transfer Window it's "the maximum number of Transfers that can be simultaneously in progress" — a cap on how many reassembly contexts a receiver holds open at once, and it slides off the Transfer Numbers carried in the segment-bearing Messages. A Bundle Message isn't a Transfer, carries no Transfer Number, and opens no reassembly state, so it occupies no window slot. That's exactly why you can send any number of Bundle Messages without touching the window: they're a stream of complete, stateless bundles. The "infinite Bundle Messages vs. finite window" comparison is really comparing two different things — stateless complete-bundle delivery versus the bounded set of concurrent segmented reassemblies, which is the only thing the window limits.

On new vs. repeated: for a complete bundle that's the Bundle Protocol's job, not the CL's, and it's well-defined. RFC 9171 identifies every bundle unambiguously by its source node ID and creation timestamp (and, for a fragment, the fragment offset and total ADU length), so duplicate detection is unambiguous and handled by the BPA. Because the link is one-way and lossy, a sender may intentionally repeat a Bundle Message for robustness (see §Repetition of Bundle Segments); the receiving CL simply hands each complete bundle up, and the BPA discards any duplicate by that identity. BTP-U only defines optional duplicate suppression within an in-progress Transfer (a receiver MAY ignore a Message it already holds); it deliberately doesn't deduplicate whole bundles, precisely because RFC 9171 already does.

So the receiver needs no Transfer Number on a Bundle Message, and there's no window accounting that any number of Bundle Messages could overflow.

Hope that helps?

comments.author: climoge
comments.body:
Hi,

Thank you, it helps a lot !
I spoke to the person I’m working with on the draft about this issue I had and they said to me that the Bundle Protocol can handle deduplication.
I'm solely focused on implementing the BTPU draft so I'm lacking a better understanding of the whole protocol.
Sorry for bothering you with beginner questions.

I've almost finished a first implementation draft of the BTPU in Rust with a C FFI, I will share it when it's done.

comments.author: ricktaylor
comments.body:
Hi,

No bother at all, I'm very happy to answer questions!

We would be interested in seeing your BTPU implementation as we have one in progress for Hardy as well, and some interop testing would be great!

https://github.com/ricktaylor/hardy/pull/529
